teens.question: What did Aaron wear when he entered the Most Holy Place on the Day of Atonement?
teens.option_a: His full high priestly garments with breastplate and ephod
teens.option_b: Simple holy linen garments — coat, breeches, girdle, and mitre
teens.option_c: The garments of a regular priest
teens.option_d: Only a white robe with no other garment
teens.explanation: Leviticus 16:4 — "He shall put on the holy linen coat, and he shall have the linen breeches upon his flesh, and shall be girded with a linen girdle, and with the linen mitre shall he be attired: these are holy garments; therefore shall he wash his flesh in water, and so put them on."

teens.question: What did Aaron use to fill the Most Holy Place with a cloud before approaching the mercy seat?
teens.option_a: Incense on a censer of fire taken from the altar
teens.option_b: Burning wood from the altar
teens.option_c: Oil poured on the mercy seat
teens.option_d: Prayers offered at the door of the tabernacle
teens.explanation: Leviticus 16:12-13 — "And he shall take a censer full of burning coals of fire from off the altar before the LORD, and his hands full of sweet incense beaten small, and bring it within the vail: and he shall put the incense upon the fire before the LORD, that the cloud of the incense may cover the mercy seat."

teens.question: What was the name given to the goat sent into the wilderness bearing Israel's sins?
teens.option_a: The sacrificial goat
teens.option_b: The scapegoat (Azazel)
teens.option_c: The atonement goat
teens.option_d: The wandering goat
teens.explanation: Leviticus 16:8 — "And Aaron shall cast lots upon the two goats; one lot for the LORD, and the other lot for the scapegoat." The Hebrew is la-Azazel, rendered "scapegoat" in the KJV.

teens.question: How often was the Day of Atonement to be observed?
teens.option_a: Every month
teens.option_b: Once a year, on the tenth day of the seventh month
teens.option_c: Twice a year at the new moon
teens.option_d: Only in years of Jubilee
teens.explanation: Leviticus 16:29 — "And this shall be a statute for ever unto you: that in the seventh month, on the tenth day of the month, ye shall afflict your souls, and do no work at all."

adults.question: The chapter opens with reference to the death of Aaron's two sons. What is the theological function of this opening in Leviticus 16?
adults.option_a: It is a historical aside
adults.option_b: It establishes the danger of improper approach to God, motivating the precise regulations that follow for the Day of Atonement
adults.option_c: It blames Aaron for their deaths
adults.option_d: It introduces a new law about fire
adults.explanation: Leviticus 16:1 — "And the LORD spake unto Moses after the death of the two sons of Aaron, when they offered before the LORD, and died." The deaths of Nadab and Abihu (Leviticus 10:1-2) frame the Day of Atonement laws as matters of life and death.

adults.question: The Hebrew word Azazel in Leviticus 16:8 has been interpreted in several ways. What are the main scholarly interpretations?
adults.option_a: Only as the name of a desert demon
adults.option_b: As "complete removal" (the goal), a rocky cliff, or a desert demon — all pointing to permanent removal of sin from the community
adults.option_c: Only as the name of the high priest who carried the goat
adults.option_d: Only as the word for "wilderness"
adults.explanation: Leviticus 16:8 — Azazel (Hebrew: la-Azazel) has been interpreted as: (1) "complete removal" or "entire sending away" of sin, (2) a specific desolate location, (3) a fallen angel/demon figure. All interpretations converge on the goat carrying sin to a place of permanent separation.

adults.question: What does the double application of blood — first for Aaron and his household, then for the people — reveal about the Day of Atonement's scope?
adults.option_a: Only the people needed atonement, not the priests
adults.option_b: Both priesthood and people needed atonement; even the mediator required cleansing before he could make atonement for others
adults.option_c: Aaron made atonement for himself only as a formality
adults.option_d: The double application doubled the effectiveness
adults.explanation: Leviticus 16:11,15 — Aaron first sacrifices a bull for himself and his household (v.11), then a goat for the congregation (v.15). This shows that even the high priest was a sinner who needed atonement — a contrast to Hebrews 7:27 where Jesus "needeth not daily to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins."

adults.question: How does Hebrews 9 use Leviticus 16 to argue for the superiority of Christ's atonement?
adults.option_a: It argues that Leviticus 16 was completely unrelated to Christ
adults.option_b: The annual, repeated entry of the high priest into the earthly Holy of Holies is contrasted with Christ's once-for-all entry into heaven's true sanctuary with His own blood
adults.option_c: Hebrews 9 says the Day of Atonement never had any value
adults.option_d: Hebrews 9 cites Leviticus 16 only as a historical record
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:7,12 — "But into the second went the high priest alone once every year, not without blood...But Christ being come an high priest...by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place, having obtained eternal redemption for us."

adults.question: The mercy seat (kapporeth) in Leviticus 16 was where blood was sprinkled seven times. What is the etymological and theological significance of kapporeth?
adults.option_a: It means "golden lid" referring to its material
adults.option_b: It derives from the root kapar (to cover/atone), making the mercy seat literally the place of covering or atonement
adults.option_c: It means "resting place" because the ark rested there
adults.option_d: It derives from the word for "meeting"
adults.explanation: Leviticus 16:14-15 — The kapporeth (mercy seat) derives from kapar, the same root as kippur in Yom Kippur. The sprinkling of blood on the mercy seat was the climactic act of atonement — God's throne became the place of covering and forgiveness.

adults.question: The Day of Atonement required Israel to "afflict your souls" (Leviticus 16:29). What did this phrase entail according to rabbinic tradition and broader biblical usage?
adults.option_a: It meant to feel sad for one day
adults.option_b: It meant fasting and self-denial, including abstaining from food, drink, and other bodily pleasures
adults.option_c: It meant wearing sackcloth only
adults.option_d: It meant confessing sins publicly to the priest
adults.explanation: Leviticus 16:29 — "ye shall afflict your souls" (Hebrew: anah nefesh) refers to self-denial, primarily fasting. Isaiah 58:3-5 confirms this: "Wherefore have we fasted, say they...Behold, ye fast for strife." The day was a solemn Sabbath of rest and self-denial.

adults.question: What happened to all the furnishings of the sanctuary and the altar on the Day of Atonement, and what does this reveal?
adults.option_a: Only the ark was cleansed
adults.option_b: The tabernacle, its furnishings, the altar, and the priesthood were all atoned for — the entire worship system needed annual purification
adults.option_c: Only the people were atoned for, not the objects
adults.option_d: The cleansing of the altar was done on a different day
adults.explanation: Leviticus 16:16-20 — "he shall make an atonement for the holy place...And he shall make an atonement for the tabernacle of the congregation, and for the altar." The entire sacred space accumulated impurity from Israel's sins and required annual cleansing — the whole covenant system needed renewal.

adults.question: The two goats of the Day of Atonement have been identified typologically as representing two aspects of Christ's atonement. What are these two aspects?
adults.option_a: Justification and sanctification
adults.option_b: Propitiation (the slain goat — satisfying God's wrath) and expiation (the scapegoat — removing sin from the sinner)
adults.option_c: The old covenant and the new covenant
adults.option_d: Judgment and mercy as separate acts
adults.explanation: Leviticus 16:15-22 — The slain goat, whose blood was brought into the Holy of Holies (v.15), pictures propitiation — satisfying divine justice. The scapegoat, bearing confessed sins into the wilderness (v.22), pictures expiation — the removal of sin's guilt from the sinner. Christ's one sacrifice accomplished both (Romans 3:25; Hebrews 9:26).

kids.question: Where were the Israelites required to bring their animals to be killed?
kids.option_b: To the door of the tabernacle
kids.option_c: Outside the camp in the wilderness
kids.explanation: Leviticus 17:3-4 — "What man soever there be of the house of Israel, that killeth an ox, or lamb, or goat, in the camp, or that killeth it out of the camp, and bringeth it not unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, to offer an offering unto the LORD before the tabernacle of the LORD; blood shall be imputed unto that man."

kids.question: What does the Bible say the life of the flesh is in?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 17:11 — "For the life of the flesh is in the blood."

kids.question: Were the Israelites allowed to eat blood?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 17:12 — "Therefore I said unto the children of Israel, No soul of you shall eat blood, neither shall any stranger that sojourneth among you eat blood."

teens.question: What was the penalty for an Israelite who sacrificed an animal outside the tabernacle, even in the field?
teens.option_a: He paid a fine to the priests
teens.option_b: He was cut off from his people
teens.option_c: He was given thirty lashes
teens.option_d: He had to offer double at the tabernacle
teens.explanation: Leviticus 17:4 — "blood shall be imputed unto that man; he hath shed blood; and that man shall be cut off from among his people."

teens.question: Why did God forbid sacrifices "in the open field" apart from the tabernacle?
teens.option_a: To prevent accidents in the field
teens.option_b: To stop the Israelites from offering sacrifices to devils (goat idols, se'irim)
teens.option_c: Because the ground in the field was unclean
teens.option_d: So the priests could count all the animals
teens.explanation: Leviticus 17:7 — "And they shall no more offer their sacrifices unto devils, after whom they have gone a whoring. This shall be a statute for ever unto them throughout their generations."

teens.question: What reason does God give in Leviticus 17:11 for the blood being on the altar for atonement?
teens.option_a: Because blood is valuable and should not be wasted
teens.option_b: Because God has given the blood upon the altar to make atonement for souls, since the life is in the blood
teens.option_c: Because blood purifies the altar
teens.option_d: Because blood was a sign of the covenant with Abraham
teens.explanation: Leviticus 17:11 — "For the life of the flesh is in the blood: and I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement for your souls: for it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul."

teens.question: What were Israelites required to do if they killed an animal or bird while hunting?
teens.option_a: Bring it to the tabernacle for inspection
teens.option_b: Pour out its blood and cover it with dust
teens.option_c: Give it to the priests
teens.option_d: Offer it as a burnt offering
teens.explanation: Leviticus 17:13 — "And whatsoever man there be of the children of Israel, or of the strangers that sojourn among you, which hunteth and catcheth any beast or fowl that may be eaten; he shall even pour out the blood thereof, and cover it with dust."

teens.question: What was required of someone who ate an animal that died of itself or was torn by beasts?
teens.option_a: They were put to death
teens.option_b: They became unclean and had to wash their clothes and bathe, remaining unclean until evening
teens.option_c: They had to offer a sin offering
teens.option_d: They were cut off from Israel
teens.explanation: Leviticus 17:15 — "And every soul that eateth that which died of itself, or that which was torn with beasts, whether it be one of your own country, or a stranger, he shall both wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even: then shall he be clean."

adults.question: The Hebrew word se'irim in Leviticus 17:7, translated "devils" in the KJV, literally means what?
adults.option_a: Evil spirits in general
adults.option_b: Hairy ones or goat-demons — a reference to goat-idol worship
adults.option_c: Fallen angels
adults.option_d: Foreign gods like Baal
adults.explanation: Leviticus 17:7 — "they shall no more offer their sacrifices unto devils" (se'irim, hairy ones). This refers to goat idols or goat-demons, possibly related to the Canaanite worship Israel had adopted or been tempted to adopt in Egypt.

adults.question: Leviticus 17:11 is a foundational text for understanding biblical atonement. What three things does it state about blood?
adults.option_a: It is sacred, holy, and divine
adults.option_b: The life of the flesh is in the blood; God has given it upon the altar; it makes atonement for the soul
adults.option_c: It must be poured, burned, and buried
adults.option_d: It is the sign of the covenant, the seal of the law, and the mark of Israel
adults.explanation: Leviticus 17:11 — "For the life of the flesh is in the blood: and I have given it to you upon the altar to make an atonement for your souls: for it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul." These three clauses give the ontological basis (life is in blood), the divine authorisation (I have given it), and the soteriological function (it makes atonement).

adults.question: How does Hebrews 9:22 quote the principle of Leviticus 17:11?
adults.option_a: By saying the law of Moses was obsolete
adults.option_b: "Without shedding of blood is no remission"
adults.option_c: "The blood of bulls cannot cleanse the conscience"
adults.option_d: "Christ abolished the blood sacrifices"
adults.explanation: Hebrews 9:22 — "And almost all things are by the law purged with blood; and without shedding of blood is no remission." This directly draws on Leviticus 17:11's principle that the blood on the altar is the divinely appointed means of atonement.

adults.question: The centralisation of sacrifice at the tabernacle in Leviticus 17 was temporary in what sense? What adjustment was made in Deuteronomy for the settlement in Canaan?
adults.option_a: There was no adjustment; the law was permanent
adults.option_b: Deuteronomy 12:15 permitted the slaughter of animals for food (not sacrifice) anywhere, while still requiring sacrificial offerings at the central sanctuary
adults.option_c: Deuteronomy abolished all animal slaughter
adults.option_d: Deuteronomy permitted sacrifice at any Levitical city
adults.explanation: Leviticus 17:3-4 required all animal slaughter to be at the tabernacle — practical for the wilderness camp. Deuteronomy 12:15 modifies this for settled life: "thou mayest kill and eat flesh in all thy gates." The distinction between sacrificial and non-sacrificial slaughter was thus introduced as Israel spread across the land.

adults.question: What is the scope of the blood prohibition in Leviticus 17 — did it apply only to Israelites?
adults.option_a: Yes, only to native Israelites
adults.option_b: No, it applied equally to strangers sojourning among Israel
adults.option_c: Only to males over thirteen years of age
adults.option_d: Only to those who had been circumcised
adults.explanation: Leviticus 17:10 — "And whatsoever man there be of the house of Israel, or of the strangers that sojourn among you, that eateth any manner of blood; I will even set my face against that soul that eateth blood." The prohibition was universal within the covenant community, including resident aliens.

adults.question: The Jerusalem Council in Acts 15 included "abstaining from blood" in its requirements for Gentile believers. How does this connect to Leviticus 17?
adults.option_a: It has no connection to Leviticus 17
adults.option_b: The council drew on the Noahide/sojourner provisions (cf. Leviticus 17:10) as a baseline for Gentile conduct in mixed Jewish-Gentile communities
adults.option_c: The council rejected all of Leviticus's food laws
adults.option_d: It was only a temporary cultural accommodation
adults.explanation: Acts 15:20 — "But that we write unto them, that they abstain from pollutions of idols, and from fornication, and from things strangled, and from blood." This parallels the Leviticus 17 provisions for sojourners, suggesting the council applied the sojourner ethic to Gentile believers in Jewish-majority communities.

adults.question: What is the theological connection between the life being in the blood (Leviticus 17:11) and the Lord's Supper in the New Testament?
adults.option_a: There is no connection
adults.option_b: Jesus's words "This is my blood...shed for many for the remission of sins" (Matthew 26:28) consciously invoke the principle that life is in blood, presenting His blood as the ultimate atonement
adults.option_c: The Lord's Supper replaced the blood prohibition of Leviticus
adults.option_d: Jesus's words meant the grape juice was transformed into blood
adults.explanation: Leviticus 17:11 — "it is the blood that maketh an atonement for the soul." Matthew 26:28 — "For this is my blood of the new testament, which is shed for many for the remission of sins." Jesus consciously invokes the Levitical principle: His blood, containing His life, is the final atoning sacrifice.

adults.question: What does the covering of blood with dust after hunting (Leviticus 17:13) signify about the Israelite view of animal life?
adults.option_a: It was a hygienic practice only
adults.option_b: The blood, representing life, was returned to the earth from which life came — acknowledging God as the giver of all life and the owner of all blood
adults.option_c: It was to hide the evidence of the kill
adults.option_d: It was a form of offering to the earth
adults.explanation: Leviticus 17:13-14 — "he shall...pour out the blood thereof, and cover it with dust. For it is the life of all flesh; the blood of it is for the life thereof." The act of covering blood with dust acknowledged that the life belonged to God and was being returned to His creation, not casually discarded.

kids.question: Whose ways were the Israelites told NOT to follow at the start of Leviticus 18?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 18:3 — "After the doings of the land of Egypt, wherein ye dwelt, shall ye not do: and after the doings of the land of Canaan, whither I bring you, shall ye not do: neither shall ye walk in their ordinances."

kids.question: Was it allowed to give your children to Molech?
kids.option_c: Only in times of great danger
kids.option_d: Only the firstborn could be given
kids.explanation: Leviticus 18:21 — "And thou shalt not let any of thy seed pass through the fire to Molech, neither shalt thou profane the name of thy God: I am the LORD."

kids.question: What does God say the land would do if the people broke all these laws?
kids.option_b: The land would vomit them out
kids.explanation: Leviticus 18:28 — "That the land spue not you out also, when ye defile it, as it spued out the nations that were before you."

teens.question: What was the repeated phrase God used to introduce the laws in Leviticus 18 and throughout the chapter?
teens.option_a: "Thus saith Moses"
teens.option_b: "I am the LORD your God"
teens.option_c: "Hear, O Israel"
teens.option_d: "The LORD thy God commands thee"
teens.explanation: Leviticus 18:2 — "Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, I am the LORD your God." This divine self-identification (repeated in vv. 4, 5, 6, 21, 30) grounds all the laws in the identity and authority of God.

teens.question: What does Leviticus 18:5 say about the man who keeps God's statutes and judgments?
teens.option_a: "He will be blessed in the land"
teens.option_b: "He shall live in them"
teens.option_c: "He will find peace with God"
teens.option_d: "He shall receive great reward"
teens.explanation: Leviticus 18:5 — "Ye shall therefore keep my statutes, and my judgments: which if a man do, he shall live in them: I am the LORD."

teens.question: Leviticus 18 lists many forbidden sexual relations. What is the general term used for these forbidden relationships?
teens.option_a: Abominations
teens.option_b: Uncovering nakedness
teens.option_c: Walking in the ordinances of the Canaanites
teens.option_d: Defiling the land
teens.explanation: Leviticus 18:6 — "None of you shall approach to any that is near of kin to him, to uncover their nakedness: I am the LORD." The phrase "uncover nakedness" is the technical term used throughout the chapter for these sexual unions.

teens.question: Why does God say the Canaanites were being driven out of the land?
teens.option_a: Because they were too numerous
teens.option_b: Because they had committed all these abominations and defiled the land
teens.option_c: Because God had promised the land to Abraham only
teens.option_d: Because they had refused to keep the Sabbath
teens.explanation: Leviticus 18:24-25 — "Defile not ye yourselves in any of these things: for in all these the nations are defiled which I cast out before you: and the land is defiled: therefore I do visit the iniquity thereof upon it."

teens.question: What is the word used in Leviticus 18 to describe same-sex relations and relations with animals?
teens.option_a: Sin
teens.option_b: Abomination and confusion
teens.option_c: Unclean
teens.option_d: Transgression
teens.explanation: Leviticus 18:22-23 — "Thou shalt not lie with mankind, as with womankind: it is abomination. Neither shalt thou lie with any beast to defile thyself therewith: neither shall any woman stand before a beast to lie down thereto: it is confusion."

adults.question: Leviticus 18:5 — "He shall live in them" — is quoted twice in the New Testament. By whom and in what context?
adults.option_a: By Peter and John
adults.option_b: By Paul in Romans 10:5 and Galatians 3:12, contrasting law-keeping as a means of life with faith
adults.option_c: By Jesus in the Sermon on the Mount
adults.option_d: By the writer of Hebrews
adults.explanation: Romans 10:5 — "Moses describeth the righteousness which is of the law, That the man which doeth those things shall live by them." Galatians 3:12 — "And the law is not of faith: but, The man that doeth them shall live in them." Paul cites Leviticus 18:5 to argue that law-righteousness and faith-righteousness are distinct principles.

adults.question: What is the significance of the phrase "I am the LORD" appearing six times in Leviticus 18?
adults.option_a: It is a liturgical refrain with no specific significance
adults.option_b: It anchors every prohibition in the covenant character of God — these are not arbitrary cultural rules but expressions of divine holiness
adults.option_c: It identifies each law as a separate commandment
adults.option_d: It signals that each law is more important than the previous one
adults.explanation: The sevenfold repetition of "I am the LORD" (vv.2,4,5,6,21,30) grounds the sexual ethics of Leviticus 18 not in natural law alone but in the personal identity of the God who redeemed Israel from Egypt and entered into covenant with them.

adults.question: The prohibition against giving seed to Molech (Leviticus 18:21) appears in the middle of sexual prohibitions. What connection does this placement suggest?
adults.option_a: It is out of place and should be in another chapter
adults.option_b: Child sacrifice was connected to the same realm of violations against God's gift of life and family — both sexual sin and Molech worship destroyed the seed and household God intended to be holy
adults.option_c: It is an insertion from a different source
adults.option_d: It only relates to agricultural seed, not children
adults.explanation: Leviticus 18:21 — The placement of the Molech prohibition within the sexual ethics section links the gift of progeny (seed) to God's purposes. To give seed to Molech was to destroy life God had given, violating both the sanctity of human life and the name of God ("neither shalt thou profane the name of thy God").

adults.question: What does the metaphor of the land "vomiting out" its inhabitants (Leviticus 18:25,28) communicate theologically?
adults.option_a: The land was populated by demons who rejected people
adults.option_b: Sexual and religious sins were so fundamentally disordering that even the created order (the land) was treated as rejecting those who violated God's ordinances
adults.option_c: It was a figure of speech with no literal meaning
adults.option_d: It referred to volcanic activity in Canaan
adults.explanation: Leviticus 18:25 — "And the land is defiled: therefore I do visit the iniquity thereof upon it, and the land itself vomiteth out her inhabitants." The metaphor personifies the land as sickened by moral pollution, reflecting the Hebrew understanding that creation itself was ordered by God's law and experienced disorder when that law was violated.

adults.question: Leviticus 18 begins by rejecting both Egyptian and Canaanite practices. What does this double rejection reveal about the nature of the laws?
adults.option_a: Israel had already adopted Egyptian practices that needed correction
adults.option_b: The laws define a unique covenant identity — Israel was not to conform to the culture they left or the culture they were entering
adults.option_c: The laws were negotiated between Moses and the Egyptians
adults.option_d: Only Canaanite practices were truly sinful
adults.explanation: Leviticus 18:3 — "After the doings of the land of Egypt...shall ye not do: and after the doings of the land of Canaan...shall ye not do." By naming both civilisations, God constitutes Israel as a third way — a holy nation whose ethics are derived from His character, not from cultural context.

adults.question: The incest prohibitions in Leviticus 18:7-18 cover many degrees of relationship. What structural principle organises these prohibitions?
adults.option_a: They are listed randomly
adults.option_b: They move outward from the closest nuclear family (parents) to extended family (aunt, sister-in-law), using "uncover the nakedness of" as the consistent legal phrase
adults.option_c: They list only female relatives
adults.option_d: They are organised alphabetically in Hebrew
adults.explanation: Leviticus 18:7-18 moves systematically: mother (v.7), father's other wife (v.8), full/half sister (vv.9,11), granddaughter (v.10), father's sister (v.12), mother's sister (v.13), father's brother's wife (v.14), daughter-in-law (v.15), brother's wife (v.16), woman and her daughter (v.17), wife's sister as rival wife (v.18). The consistent phrase "uncover nakedness" structures each case.

adults.question: Paul's condemnation of sexual immorality in 1 Corinthians 5:1 references a specific case of incest. How does this relate to Leviticus 18?
adults.option_a: Paul invented this prohibition without reference to the law
adults.option_b: Paul condemns a man who has his father's wife, exactly the case prohibited in Leviticus 18:8, noting it was "not so much as named among the Gentiles"
adults.option_c: Paul cited this as an example of the law being abolished
adults.option_d: Paul was addressing a unique situation not found in the law
adults.explanation: 1 Corinthians 5:1 — "It is reported commonly that there is fornication among you...that one should have his father's wife." This precisely matches Leviticus 18:8 — "The nakedness of thy father's wife shalt thou not uncover: it is thy father's nakedness." Paul's moral reasoning draws directly on the Levitical framework.

adults.question: How does Leviticus 18:24-25 function as a theological explanation for the conquest of Canaan?
adults.option_a: It presents the conquest as ethnic cleansing with no moral justification
adults.option_b: It frames the expulsion of the Canaanites as divine judgment for specific enumerated sins, not ethnic preference, making the conquest a morally coherent act of justice
adults.option_c: It suggests the Canaanites were expelled for racial reasons
adults.option_d: It is only a warning to Israel with no reference to Canaan's fate
adults.explanation: Leviticus 18:24-25 — "Defile not ye yourselves...for in all these the nations are defiled which I cast out before you: and the land is defiled: therefore I do visit the iniquity thereof upon it." The text makes explicit that Canaan's expulsion was moral judgment for the sins catalogued in vv.6-23 — the same sins now forbidden to Israel. The conquest is thus presented as judicial, not arbitrary.

kids.question: What command did God give to Israel at the very beginning of Leviticus 19?
kids.option_b: "Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am holy"
kids.explanation: Leviticus 19:2 — "Speak unto all the congregation of the children of Israel, and say unto them, Ye shall be holy: for I the LORD your God am holy."

kids.question: What were farmers commanded to leave in their fields for the poor?
kids.option_b: The corners of the field and the gleanings for the poor and stranger
kids.explanation: Leviticus 19:9-10 — "And when ye reap the harvest of your land, thou shalt not wholly reap the corners of thy field, neither shalt thou gather the gleanings of thy harvest...thou shalt leave them for the poor and stranger: I am the LORD your God."

kids.question: What is the great commandment about neighbours that Jesus later called the second great commandment?
kids.option_b: "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself"
kids.explanation: Leviticus 19:18 — "Thou shalt not avenge, nor bear any grudge against the children of thy people, but thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself: I am the LORD."

teens.question: Leviticus 19 lists many practical holiness laws. Which of the following is commanded in this chapter?
teens.option_a: "Thou shalt make no idols"
teens.option_b: "Thou shalt rise up before the hoary head, and honour the face of the old man"
teens.option_c: "Thou shalt not eat of the fat of the animal"
teens.option_d: "Thou shalt offer a burnt offering on the Sabbath"
teens.explanation: Leviticus 19:32 — "Thou shalt rise up before the hoary head, and honour the face of the old man, and fear thy God: I am the LORD."

teens.question: What does Leviticus 19 command about dealing with the deaf and blind?
teens.option_a: "Teach them and care for them"
teens.option_b: "Thou shalt not curse the deaf, nor put a stumblingblock before the blind"
teens.option_c: "Build them a safe place in the camp"
teens.option_d: "Appoint Levites to care for them"
teens.explanation: Leviticus 19:14 — "Thou shalt not curse the deaf, nor put a stumblingblock before the blind, but shalt fear thy God: I am the LORD."

teens.question: What does Leviticus 19:15 command about justice in judgment?
teens.option_a: "Favour the poor because they are needy"
teens.option_b: "Thou shalt not do unrighteousness in judgment: thou shalt not respect the person of the poor, nor honour the person of the mighty; but in righteousness shalt thou judge thy neighbour"
teens.option_c: "Let the priest decide all cases"
teens.option_d: "Give the benefit of the doubt to the rich"
teens.explanation: Leviticus 19:15 — "Ye shall do no unrighteousness in judgment: thou shalt not respect the person of the poor, nor honour the person of the mighty: but in righteousness shalt thou judge thy neighbour."

teens.question: What does Leviticus 19 say about mixing two kinds of seed, wearing garments of mixed linen and wool, or plowing with two different animals?
teens.option_a: These were encouraged as signs of diversity
teens.option_b: These were all forbidden as diverse kinds (sha'atnez)
teens.option_c: Only wearing mixed cloth was forbidden
teens.option_d: These were customs from Egypt to be avoided
teens.explanation: Leviticus 19:19 — "Thou shalt not let thy cattle gender with a diverse kind: thou shalt not sow thy field with mingled seed: neither shall a garment mingled of linen and woollen come upon thee."

teens.question: What law about the stranger is given in Leviticus 19:33-34?
teens.option_a: "Be polite to strangers but do not eat with them"
teens.option_b: "The stranger that dwelleth with you shall be as one born among you, and thou shalt love him as thyself"
teens.option_c: "Strangers must follow only the food laws"
teens.option_d: "Test strangers before welcoming them"
teens.explanation: Leviticus 19:34 — "But the stranger that dwelleth with you shall be unto you as one born among you, and thou shalt love him as thyself; for ye were strangers in the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God."

adults.question: Leviticus 19:18 contains the command to love one's neighbour as oneself. How did Jesus use this verse in the New Testament?
adults.option_a: He quoted it as the least of the commandments
adults.option_b: He declared it the second great commandment, equal to loving God, and said all the law and prophets hang on these two
adults.option_c: He said it was only for Israel, not for Gentiles
adults.option_d: He replaced it with a new commandment
adults.explanation: Matthew 22:39 — "And the second is like unto it, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself." Matthew 22:40 — "On these two commandments hang all the law and the prophets." Jesus directly cites Leviticus 19:18 and elevates it to the heart of the entire Mosaic ethic.

adults.question: The phrase "I am the LORD" appears approximately fifteen times in Leviticus 19. What is the rhetorical and theological function of this repetition?
adults.option_a: It is a scribal error repeated through copying
adults.option_b: Each law is individually grounded in the covenant identity of God — obedience is not to an abstract code but to the personal God who redeemed Israel
adults.option_c: It marks the laws that were specifically for priests
adults.option_d: It indicates which laws could be modified by Israel's leaders
adults.explanation: Leviticus 19:2-37 — The repeated "I am the LORD" (approximately 15 occurrences) does not mark a section heading but closes each individual law or cluster with a divine claim. This personalises the law, making each statute a direct expression of the character of the God who says "I am the LORD."

adults.question: What is the sha'atnez law (Leviticus 19:19; Deuteronomy 22:11) and how has it been interpreted theologically?
adults.option_a: It is a law against wearing too many layers of clothing
adults.option_b: The prohibition of mixing wool and linen has been interpreted as a holiness boundary — maintaining the distinctions God created, or as reserving the priestly garments (which combined both) for their unique sacred function
adults.option_c: It was purely a health code against skin irritation
adults.option_d: It was borrowed from Egyptian customs
adults.explanation: Leviticus 19:19 — "neither shall a garment mingled of linen and woollen come upon thee." Deuteronomy 22:11 — "Thou shalt not wear a garment of divers sorts, as of woollen and linen together." Priestly garments combined linen and wool (Exodus 28:6,8,15), so one interpretation is that the prohibition reserved this mixture for the holy priesthood, maintaining boundaries between the sacred and common.

adults.question: Leviticus 19:26-28 prohibits divination, enchantment, rounding the corners of the head, and cutting marks in the flesh. What do these prohibitions have in common?
adults.option_a: They are arbitrary cultural restrictions
adults.option_b: They are practices associated with pagan mourning rites and occult communication — all were forms of seeking knowledge or power outside the covenant with God
adults.option_c: They were forbidden for hygienic reasons
adults.option_d: They were practices Moses personally disliked
adults.explanation: Leviticus 19:26-28 — "Ye shall not eat any thing with the blood: neither shall ye use enchantment, nor observe times...Ye shall not make any cuttings in your flesh for the dead, nor print any marks upon you." These practices were associated with communication with the dead, seeking occult wisdom, and pagan mourning rituals — all violations of exclusive covenant fidelity to God.

adults.question: The gleaning laws (Leviticus 19:9-10) are illustrated in the book of Ruth. What theological principle do they embody?
adults.option_a: Charity was optional in Israel
adults.option_b: The produce of the land ultimately belonged to God; Israel's ownership was stewardship, and the poor had a God-given right to a portion of the harvest
adults.option_c: The gleaning laws were only for Levites
adults.option_d: Poor people were required to work before receiving any food
adults.explanation: Leviticus 19:9-10 — "thou shalt leave them for the poor and stranger: I am the LORD your God." The gleaning laws embedded the rights of the poor into the agricultural economy, reflecting the principle that the land and its produce belonged to God (Leviticus 25:23) and were held in trust for all members of the covenant community.

adults.question: What is the Orla law in Leviticus 19:23-25 and what does it teach about patience and covenant faithfulness?
adults.option_a: The law against eating the first grain after the Sabbath
adults.option_b: The law forbidding eating the fruit of newly planted trees for three years; the fourth year fruit was holy to the LORD; only in the fifth year was it for personal use
adults.option_c: The law against cutting down fruit trees in war
adults.option_d: The law about planting on the Sabbath year
adults.explanation: Leviticus 19:23-25 — "And when ye shall come into the land, and shall have planted all manner of trees for food...three years shall it be as uncircumcised unto you...in the fourth year all the fruit thereof shall be holy to praise the LORD...and in the fifth year shall ye eat of the fruit thereof." The Orla law taught Israel to wait, surrender the first increase to God, and trust His provision.

adults.question: How does Leviticus 19:17-18 present the internal heart requirement of the law, in contrast to merely external behaviour?
adults.option_a: It only regulates external actions
adults.option_b: "Thou shalt not hate thy brother in thine heart" (v.17) addresses the interior disposition, making hatred and grudge-bearing violations of the law alongside outward acts
adults.option_c: It only concerns the treatment of enemies
adults.option_d: The verse is only about community dispute resolution
adults.explanation: Leviticus 19:17-18 — "Thou shalt not hate thy brother in thine heart...thou shalt not avenge, nor bear any grudge...but thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself." The law reaches into the heart (hatred, grudges) not merely external behaviour, anticipating Jesus's Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:21-22) in demanding interior righteousness.

adults.question: James 2:8 calls the command to love one's neighbour "the royal law." How does this designation connect to Leviticus 19:18 and the overall argument of James?
adults.option_a: James uses it as an example of a law that can be broken without consequence
adults.option_b: By calling it the "royal law," James elevates Leviticus 19:18 as the kingly standard by which all social behaviour is judged — showing partiality violates this royal law and makes one a transgressor
adults.option_c: James argues this law replaced all of Leviticus
adults.option_d: James uses the phrase "royal law" to mean a law only for kings
adults.explanation: James 2:8 — "If ye fulfil the royal law according to the scripture, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself, ye do well." James argues that showing partiality to the rich (James 2:1-4) violates this royal law of Leviticus 19:18, demonstrating that the Levitical ethics remained normative for the New Testament church.

kids.question: What was the punishment for anyone who gave their children to the false god Molech?
kids.option_a: They were sent out of the camp
kids.option_c: They had to pay a large offering
kids.option_d: They were made to be servants
kids.explanation: Leviticus 20:2 — "Whosoever he be of the children of Israel, or of the strangers that sojourn in Israel, that giveth any of his seed unto Molech; he shall surely be put to death: the people of the land shall stone him with stones."

kids.question: What was God's punishment for someone who went to a medium or wizard?
kids.option_a: He would bless them with much less
kids.option_b: God would set His face against that person and cut them off
kids.option_c: They had to bring a special offering
kids.explanation: Leviticus 20:6 — "And the soul that turneth after such as have familiar spirits, and after wizards, to go a whoring after them, I will even set my face against that soul, and will cut him off from among his people."

kids.question: What does God say He has done to separate Israel from other peoples?
kids.option_b: He has severed them from other people that they should be His
kids.option_c: He gave them more laws to follow
kids.option_d: He made them stronger than all nations
kids.explanation: Leviticus 20:26 — "And ye shall be holy unto me: for I the LORD am holy, and have severed you from other people, that ye should be mine."

teens.question: If the community of Israel looked the other way and did nothing when a man gave his child to Molech, what did God say He would do?
teens.option_a: Nothing, since the community was innocent
teens.option_b: He would set His face against that man and his family and cut them off
teens.option_c: He would send a plague on the community
teens.option_d: He would raise up enemies against Israel
teens.explanation: Leviticus 20:4-5 — "And if the people of the land do any ways hide their eyes from the man, when he giveth of his seed unto Molech, and kill him not: then I will set my face against that man, and against his family, and will cut him off."

teens.question: What was the punishment for cursing one's father or mother according to Leviticus 20?
teens.option_a: They were beaten and made to apologise
teens.option_b: His blood shall be upon him — they were put to death
teens.option_c: They were exiled for thirty days
teens.option_d: They lost their inheritance
teens.explanation: Leviticus 20:9 — "For every one that curseth his father or his mother shall be surely put to death: he hath cursed his father or his mother; his blood shall be upon him."

teens.question: What phrase does Leviticus 20 repeatedly use for sins committed between people, indicating personal accountability?
teens.option_a: "Their iniquity be upon them"
teens.option_b: "His blood shall be upon him" / "they shall bear their iniquity"
teens.option_c: "The LORD will judge them"
teens.option_d: "Let them be cast out of the assembly"
teens.explanation: Leviticus 20:9,11,12,13,16 etc. — The repeated phrases "his blood shall be upon him" and "they shall bear their iniquity" emphasise that those who commit these sins take personal responsibility for the consequences of their actions.

teens.question: What distinguished the clean and unclean animals, and why does Leviticus 20 mention this again at the end of the chapter?
teens.option_a: To add detail to the dietary laws
teens.option_b: God had severed Israel from the nations just as He had severed clean from unclean animals — the distinctions of diet reflected the holy/common distinction of Israel's identity
teens.option_c: The dietary laws were being updated in this chapter
teens.option_d: It was a summary for new Israelites joining the community
teens.explanation: Leviticus 20:24-26 — "I am the LORD your God, which have separated you from other people...Ye shall therefore put difference between clean beasts and unclean...and ye shall be holy unto me: for I the LORD am holy, and have severed you from other people, that ye should be mine." The dietary distinctions mirror the theological distinction of Israel's election.

teens.question: What was the fate of anyone in Israel who was a medium or wizard?
teens.option_a: They were expelled from the camp
teens.option_b: They were put to death by stoning
teens.option_c: They were placed under the authority of the priests
teens.option_d: They were given one chance to repent
teens.explanation: Leviticus 20:27 — "A man also or woman that hath a familiar spirit, or that is a wizard, shall surely be put to death: they shall stone them with stones: their blood shall be upon them."

adults.question: The phrase "I will set my face against" appears in Leviticus 20:3,5,6. What is the covenantal weight of this phrase?
adults.option_a: It is a mild expression of disappointment
adults.option_b: It is the most severe expression of divine opposition — God's active, personal, covenant-breaking hostility against the offender
adults.option_c: It means God will watch them more closely
adults.option_d: It refers to God turning away without acting
adults.explanation: Leviticus 20:3 — "I will set my face against that man." This phrase (natan panim b-) is the inverse of the Aaronic blessing's "the LORD make his face shine upon thee" (Numbers 6:25) — it represents the full weight of divine covenantal hostility, the opposite of blessing and presence.

adults.question: Leviticus 20 applies the death penalty to a range of offences. What principle guided the severity of penalties, and how were gradations of punishment distinguished?
adults.option_a: All offences received equal punishment
adults.option_b: Offences were graded from death (the most severe violations of covenant fundamentals) to being cut off to bearing iniquity without community-administered penalty
adults.option_c: The penalties were arbitrary
adults.option_d: The priests decided each case individually
adults.explanation: Leviticus 20 has three tiers: (1) death sentences (vv.2,9,10,11,12,13,15,16,27) for the gravest covenant violations; (2) being "cut off" (vv.17,18) indicating divine removal without community action; (3) "bearing iniquity" (vv.17,19,20) leaving accountability with God. This graduated system reflects proportional justice.

adults.question: Leviticus 20:17 — the case of a man seeing the nakedness of his sister and both bearing their iniquity — does not prescribe death but being "cut off in the sight of their people." What does being "cut off" (karet) mean in Hebrew law?
adults.option_a: Banishment from the camp for thirty days
adults.option_b: Excommunication from the covenant community, possibly including divine judgment of premature death
adults.option_c: Having the right hand cut off as punishment
adults.option_d: Loss of inheritance rights only
adults.explanation: The Hebrew karet (cut off) in Leviticus 20:17-18 refers to exclusion from the covenant community. Rabbinic tradition understood it as premature death by divine action when no human court acted. It represents removal from God's covenant blessings, a form of covenantal death.

adults.question: How does the command of Leviticus 20:26 — "I have severed you from other people, that ye should be mine" — function as the theological capstone of Leviticus 18-20?
adults.option_a: It is a postscript with no connection to the preceding laws
adults.option_b: It reveals that the entire holiness code of chapters 18-20 is grounded in Israel's election — their holiness is a response to their identity as God's separated people
adults.option_c: It refers only to dietary laws
adults.option_d: It addresses only Israel's relationship to Egypt
adults.explanation: Leviticus 20:26 — The word "severed" (badal) echoes the separation of light from darkness in Genesis 1:4 and the division of clean from unclean in Leviticus 20:25. Israel's election (severed from nations) is the basis and pattern for their ethical distinctiveness — they are holy because God is holy and has made them His own.

adults.question: The reference to the land flowing with milk and honey in Leviticus 20:24 appears in the context of the laws of holiness. What theological point does this juxtaposition make?
adults.option_a: The land was the reward for keeping the dietary laws only
adults.option_b: Blessing in the land (milk and honey) was inseparable from covenantal faithfulness — the land's abundance depended on Israel's holiness
adults.option_c: The description of the land was to motivate keeping only the purity laws
adults.option_d: It is a geographical note irrelevant to the laws
adults.explanation: Leviticus 20:24 — "I am the LORD your God, which have separated you from other people...I have said unto you, Ye shall inherit their land, a land that floweth with milk and honey." The promise of the land is embedded within the holiness commands, making clear that retention of the land was tied to covenantal obedience (cf. Leviticus 18:28).

adults.question: Paul quotes from Leviticus 20:26 in 2 Corinthians 6:17 — "come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord." How does this New Testament use recontextualise the Leviticus 20 principle?
adults.option_a: Paul argues Israel's separation laws are now irrelevant
adults.option_b: Paul applies the principle of separation from the nations to the call of believers to be distinct from the world — the election-holiness principle of Leviticus is reapplied to the new covenant community
adults.option_c: Paul is only addressing dietary laws
adults.option_d: Paul uses it as a proof text for physical isolation from non-believers
adults.explanation: 2 Corinthians 6:17 — "Come out from among them, and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing." Paul draws on Leviticus 20:26's principle of separation as belonging to God and applies it to the new covenant community's ethical distinctiveness in a Gentile world, showing the Levitical holiness principle transcends its original form.

adults.question: The death penalty for adultery in Leviticus 20:10 applied to both parties — "the man that committeth adultery with another man's wife." What does this symmetrical application reveal about the law?
adults.option_a: The law was biased toward women
adults.option_b: The law applied equally to both the man and the woman involved, reflecting an even-handed justice rather than holding only one party accountable
adults.option_c: Only the woman was truly at fault
adults.option_d: The penalty was rarely enforced
adults.explanation: Leviticus 20:10 — "the man that committeth adultery with another man's wife...the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death." The equal accountability of both parties stands in contrast to ancient Near Eastern laws that often punished women more severely, reflecting a principle of equal moral responsibility before God.

adults.question: Leviticus 20 ends with the command regarding mediums and wizards (v.27). How does this final verse bring Leviticus 17-20 to a thematic close?
adults.option_a: It adds a new topic unrelated to the preceding material
adults.option_b: It completes the holiness code's treatment of all rival allegiances — sexual, familial, and spiritual — by closing with the prohibition of occult consultation, which was the ultimate rival to seeking God
adults.option_c: It begins a new section on priesthood
adults.option_d: It refers only to Canaanite priests
adults.explanation: Leviticus 20:27 — The final verse on mediums and wizards echoes the opening concern of Leviticus 20 (Molech worship, v.2) and Leviticus 19:31 — "Regard not them that have familiar spirits, neither seek after wizards, to be defiled by them." Together, chapters 17-20 form the Holiness Code's comprehensive vision: Israel's loyalty to God must be total — in sacrifice (17), sexuality (18), ethics (19), and spirituality (20).

kids.question: Who were the special rules in Leviticus 21 written for?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 21:1 — "And the LORD said unto Moses, Speak unto the priests the sons of Aaron."

kids.question: Who was the most important priest that had extra holiness rules?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 21:10 — "And he that is the high priest among his brethren, upon whose head the anointing oil was poured."

kids.question: Could a priest with a physical blemish offer the burnt offerings at the altar?
kids.option_a: No, he could not offer them
kids.option_b: Yes, he could offer any offering
kids.option_d: Only with the help of another priest
kids.explanation: Leviticus 21:17 — "Whosoever he be of thy seed in their generations that hath any blemish, let him not approach to offer the bread of his God."

teens.question: For whose dead body could a priest defile himself without breaking the law?
teens.option_a: Only his mother or father
teens.option_b: His kin that is near unto him: mother, father, son, daughter, brother, and virgin sister
teens.option_c: No one — priests could never defile themselves
teens.option_d: Any Israelite from his tribe
teens.explanation: Leviticus 21:2-3 — "But for his kin, that is near unto him, that is, for his mother, and for his father, and for his son, and for his daughter, and for his brother."

teens.question: What restriction was placed on who a priest could marry?
teens.option_a: He could marry any Israelite woman
teens.option_b: He could not marry a widow, a divorced woman, a profane woman, or a harlot — only a virgin of his own people
teens.option_c: He could only marry a widow
teens.option_d: He had to marry a woman from the tribe of Levi
teens.explanation: Leviticus 21:7 — "They shall not take a wife that is a whore, or profane; neither shall they take a woman put away from her husband."

teens.question: What happened if a priest's daughter played the whore?
teens.option_a: She was cast out of the camp
teens.option_b: She was to be burnt with fire
teens.option_c: She was fined and rebuked
teens.option_d: She was given to another family
teens.explanation: Leviticus 21:9 — "And the daughter of any priest, if she profane herself by playing the whore, she profaneth her father: she shall be burnt with fire."

teens.question: What additional mourning restriction was placed on the high priest?
teens.option_a: He could not mourn at all — not even for his parents
teens.option_b: He could mourn privately but not publicly
teens.option_c: He was allowed to mourn for seven days
teens.option_d: He could only mourn on the Day of Atonement
teens.explanation: Leviticus 21:11 — "Neither shall he go in to any dead body, nor defile himself for his father, or for his mother."

teens.question: Could a priest with a blemish still eat of the holy food?
teens.option_a: Yes, he could eat the bread of his God
teens.option_b: No, blemished priests were completely excluded
teens.option_c: Only on certain feast days
teens.option_d: Only if approved by the high priest
teens.explanation: Leviticus 21:22 — "He shall eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy, and of the holy."

adults.question: What is the theological reason priests were required to maintain exceptional holiness?
adults.option_a: They were of a higher social class
adults.option_b: They offered the bread of God and were His consecrated servants — their purity represented His holiness to Israel
adults.option_c: They were simply following a priestly tradition
adults.option_d: God wanted to distinguish them from Levites visually
adults.explanation: Leviticus 21:6 — "They shall be holy unto their God, and not profane the name of their God: for the offerings of the LORD made by fire...they do offer."

adults.question: What does the phrase "profane the name of their God" in Leviticus 21:6 reveal about the stakes of priestly conduct?
adults.option_a: It means the priest would lose his salary
adults.option_b: A priest's sinful behavior brought dishonor to God's own holy name before the congregation
adults.option_c: It was a legal term for contract violation
adults.option_d: It referred only to verbal blasphemy
adults.explanation: Leviticus 21:6 — "They shall be holy unto their God, and not profane the name of their God." Priestly conduct was a public testimony to God's character.

adults.question: What specific reason does Leviticus 21:12 give for why the high priest must not leave the sanctuary?
adults.option_a: He must guard the holy objects
adults.option_b: He must not profane the sanctuary because the consecration of God's anointing oil is upon him
adults.option_c: He was too valuable to risk outside
adults.option_d: He needed to intercede continually
adults.explanation: Leviticus 21:12 — "Neither shall he go out of the sanctuary, nor profane the sanctuary of his God; for the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him."

adults.question: How many physical blemishes are listed in Leviticus 21:18-20 that disqualified a priest from approaching the altar?
adults.option_a: Five
adults.option_b: Seven
adults.option_c: Twelve
adults.option_d: Nine
adults.explanation: Leviticus 21:18-20 lists twelve blemishes including blindness, lameness, flat nose, broken foot, crooked back, scurvy, crookbacked, dwarf, blemish in eye, wen, scurvy, and broken hand.

adults.question: What typological significance does the requirement of priestly physical perfection have in light of the New Testament?
adults.option_a: It showed God valued physical appearance above character
adults.option_b: It foreshadowed Christ the perfect High Priest who had no blemish — holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners
adults.option_c: It was a cultural health code with no spiritual meaning
adults.option_d: It only applied in the wilderness period
adults.explanation: Leviticus 21:17 prefigures Hebrews 7:26 — "For such an high priest became us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners."

adults.question: The restriction that the high priest could only marry a virgin (Leviticus 21:13) carries what deeper covenantal significance?
adults.option_a: It was a practical policy to avoid inheritance disputes
adults.option_b: The high priest as a type of Christ could only be "wedded" to a pure bride — typifying Christ and the Church as a holy, undefiled bride
adults.option_c: It applied to all Israelite men in leadership
adults.option_d: It was later rescinded under the monarchy
adults.explanation: Leviticus 21:13-14 — "And he shall take a wife in her virginity." This typifies Ephesians 5:27, Christ presenting the church holy and without blemish.

adults.question: What does Leviticus 21:8 instruct the congregation regarding the priest?
adults.option_a: To obey him as if he were a king
adults.option_b: To sanctify him — thou shalt sanctify him — because he offers the bread of God
adults.option_c: To support him financially
adults.option_d: To test him before ordination
adults.explanation: Leviticus 21:8 — "Thou shalt sanctify him therefore; for he offereth the bread of thy God: he shall be holy unto thee: for I the LORD, which sanctify you, am holy."

adults.question: What does the statement "I the LORD do sanctify them" (Leviticus 21:15) reveal about the source of priestly holiness?
adults.option_a: Holiness comes from ritual observance alone
adults.option_b: Holiness is ultimately God's gift and work — the priest's sanctification is rooted in divine action, not merely human compliance
adults.option_c: It shows the high priest earned his status
adults.option_d: It was a ceremonial affirmation with no deeper meaning
adults.explanation: Leviticus 21:15 — "Neither shall he profane his seed among his people: for I the LORD do sanctify him." God is the author and sustainer of true holiness.

kids.question: Could a sick priest eat from the holy offerings?
kids.option_a: No, he had to be clean first
kids.explanation: Leviticus 22:4 — "What man soever of the seed of Aaron is a leper, or hath a running issue; he shall not eat of the holy things, until he be clean."

kids.question: What kind of animals were allowed to be offered to God?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 22:21 — "Whosoever offereth a sacrifice of peace offerings unto the LORD to accomplish his vow, or a freewill offering in beeves or sheep, it shall be perfect to be accepted; there shall be no blemish therein."

kids.question: Could a stranger who was not from a priestly family eat the holy food?
kids.option_a: No, strangers could not eat it
kids.option_b: Yes, if the priest invited them
kids.explanation: Leviticus 22:10 — "There shall no stranger eat of the holy thing: a sojourner of the priest, or an hired servant, shall not eat of the holy thing."

teens.question: Who among the priest's household could lawfully eat of the holy offerings?
teens.option_a: A bought servant born in his house, and his daughter if she had no husband
teens.option_b: Any Israelite who was a friend of the priest
teens.option_c: All Levites regardless of household
teens.option_d: Only the priest himself
teens.explanation: Leviticus 22:11-12 — "But if the priest buy any soul with his money...he shall eat of it...But the priest's daughter, if she be married unto a stranger, she may not eat of an offering of the holy things."

teens.question: If a person accidentally ate a holy thing, what were they required to do?
teens.option_a: Fast for seven days
teens.option_b: Restore the holy thing and add a fifth part to it
teens.option_c: Bring a sin offering only
teens.option_d: Be excluded from the congregation
teens.explanation: Leviticus 22:14 — "And if a man eat of the holy thing unwittingly, then he shall put the fifth part thereof unto it, and shall give it unto the priest with the holy thing."

teens.question: Could a newborn calf or lamb be offered before it was eight days old?
teens.option_a: No, it had to be at least eight days old
teens.option_b: Yes, any age was acceptable
teens.option_c: It had to be at least one month old
teens.option_d: It depended on the type of offering
teens.explanation: Leviticus 22:27 — "When a bullock, or a sheep, or a goat, is brought forth, then it shall be seven days under the dam; and from the eighth day and thenceforth it shall be accepted for an offering."

teens.question: Was it lawful to offer a mother animal and her young on the same day?
teens.option_a: No, they could not be slaughtered on the same day
teens.option_b: Yes, it was encouraged as a double offering
teens.option_c: Only if it was a freewill offering
teens.option_d: Only on the Day of Atonement
teens.explanation: Leviticus 22:28 — "Whether it be a cow or ewe, ye shall not kill it and her young both in one day."

teens.question: What defects made an animal unacceptable as an offering to God?
teens.option_a: Blindness, broken bones, maimed, having a wen, scurvy, or scab
teens.option_b: Old age or small size
teens.option_c: Wrong color or incorrect breed
teens.option_d: Being from a foreign flock
teens.explanation: Leviticus 22:22 — "Blind, or broken, or maimed, or having a wen, or scurvy, or scabbed, ye shall not offer these unto the LORD."

adults.question: What does the repeated phrase "I am the LORD" throughout Leviticus 22 underscore about the holiness laws?
adults.option_a: It is a legal formula with no deeper meaning
adults.option_b: It anchors all holiness regulations in the authority and character of God Himself — obedience is a response to who He is
adults.option_c: It identifies the source of the Levitical text
adults.option_d: It is a doxological formula borrowed from Egypt
adults.explanation: Leviticus 22:31-32 — "Therefore shall ye keep my commandments, and do them: I am the LORD...I am the LORD which hallow you."

adults.question: What does the requirement for unblemished offerings typologically point to in the New Testament?
adults.option_a: It shows God values physical perfection in worshippers
adults.option_b: It prefigures Christ as the Lamb without blemish and without spot offered for our sins
adults.option_c: It was only a sanitary measure
adults.option_d: It applied only under Mosaic law with no lasting significance
adults.explanation: Leviticus 22:21 points to 1 Peter 1:19 — "But with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot."

adults.question: What specific uncleanness conditions are listed in Leviticus 22:4-6 that prevented a priest from eating holy things?
adults.option_a: Leprosy, running issue, contact with a dead body, an emission, touching a creeping thing, or touching a man that is unclean
adults.option_b: Only leprosy and skin disease
adults.option_c: Contact with Gentiles alone
adults.option_d: Only touching the dead
adults.explanation: Leviticus 22:4-6 — "What man soever...is a leper, or hath a running issue...or a man whose seed goeth from him...whosoever toucheth any such shall be unclean."

adults.question: What does Leviticus 22:9 warn the priests will happen if they profane the holy things?
adults.option_a: They will lose their priestly inheritance
adults.option_b: They shall bear sin for it and die
adults.option_c: They will be exiled from the camp
adults.option_d: They will be fined by the congregation
adults.explanation: Leviticus 22:9 — "They shall therefore keep mine ordinance, lest they bear sin for it, and die therefore, if they profane it."

adults.question: Why was the offering of a freewill offering held to a higher standard — being perfect — while a vow-based offering allowed minor imperfections for certain animals?
adults.option_a: The Law was inconsistent in this area
adults.option_b: Freewill offerings were a pure act of devotion and required perfection; vow offerings for cattle allowed for slight redundant overgrowth but no serious blemish
adults.option_c: The high priest could waive standards for vow offerings
adults.option_d: Freewill offerings were cheaper
adults.explanation: Leviticus 22:23 — "Either a bullock or a lamb that hath any thing superfluous or lacking in his parts, that mayest thou offer for a freewill offering; but for a vow it shall not be accepted."

adults.question: What does Leviticus 22:32 identify as the goal of God's holiness laws among Israel?
adults.option_a: Political order and social cohesion
adults.option_b: That God's holy name not be profaned — "I will be hallowed among the children of Israel"
adults.option_c: Economic fairness in offerings
adults.option_d: Priestly income regulation
adults.explanation: Leviticus 22:32 — "Neither shall ye profane my holy name; but I will be hallowed among the children of Israel: I am the LORD which hallow you."

adults.question: What redemption principle does the priest's daughter illustrate in Leviticus 22:12-13?
adults.option_a: Women had no legal standing in priestly households
adults.option_b: Belonging to the priestly household determines access to holy provision — she loses access upon marrying out but regains it upon returning
adults.option_c: All daughters of priests permanently lost access upon marriage
adults.option_d: She could petition the elders for reinstatement
adults.explanation: Leviticus 22:12-13 — If married to a stranger she may not eat; but if widowed or divorced and returned to her father's house she shall eat of her father's meat.

adults.question: What is the significance of God saying "I brought you out of Egypt" at the end of Leviticus 22:33 in the context of holiness commands?
adults.option_a: It is a historical footnote
adults.option_b: The exodus is the foundational act of redemption that obligates Israel to worship a holy God — holiness flows from grace, not the reverse
adults.option_c: It refers only to the priests' ancestral history
adults.option_d: It was added by a later editor
adults.explanation: Leviticus 22:33 — "That brought you out of the land of Egypt, to be your God: I am the LORD." The Redeemer's identity grounds the call to holy offerings.

kids.question: What special day did God say was a day of rest every week?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 23:3 — "Six days shall work be done: but the seventh day is the sabbath of rest, an holy convocation."

kids.question: What feast reminded Israel of the time God brought them out of Egypt?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 23:5 — "In the fourteenth day of the first month at even is the LORD'S passover."

kids.question: During the Feast of Tabernacles, where did the Israelites live for seven days?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 23:42 — "Ye shall dwell in booths seven days; all that are Israelites born shall dwell in booths."

teens.question: What were the seven appointed feasts of the LORD listed in Leviticus 23?
teens.option_a: Sabbath, Passover, Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, Pentecost, Trumpets, Day of Atonement, and Tabernacles
teens.option_b: New Moon, Passover, Weeks, Trumpets, Atonement, Tabernacles, and Purim
teens.option_c: Passover, Weeks, Booths, Sabbath, New Year, Atonement, and Dedication
teens.option_d: Passover, Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, Pentecost, Trumpets, Atonement, and Tabernacles
teens.explanation: Leviticus 23 — The eight convocations include Sabbath (v.3), Passover (v.5), Unleavened Bread (v.6), Firstfruits (v.10), Pentecost/Weeks (v.16), Trumpets (v.24), Day of Atonement (v.27), and Tabernacles (v.34).

teens.question: On what date did the Feast of Unleavened Bread begin, and how long did it last?
teens.option_a: The 15th of the first month, for seven days
teens.option_b: The 1st of the first month, for seven days
teens.option_c: The 10th of the first month, for three days
teens.option_d: The 14th of the first month, for ten days
teens.explanation: Leviticus 23:6 — "And on the fifteenth day of the same month is the feast of unleavened bread unto the LORD: seven days ye must eat unleavened bread."

teens.question: What was the Feast of Firstfruits and when was the sheaf waved before the LORD?
teens.option_a: The first harvest was burned; waved on the first day of the third month
teens.option_b: A sheaf of the firstfruits of harvest was waved before the LORD on the day after the Sabbath following Passover
teens.option_c: The entire harvest was dedicated at the new moon
teens.option_d: It was a private ceremony performed by the high priest on Passover
teens.explanation: Leviticus 23:11 — "And he shall wave the sheaf before the LORD, to be accepted for you: on the morrow after the sabbath the priest shall wave it."

teens.question: What special observance took place on the first day of the seventh month?
teens.option_a: The Day of Atonement began
teens.option_b: A memorial of blowing of trumpets, a holy convocation
teens.option_c: The Jubilee was declared
teens.option_d: The Feast of Tabernacles started
teens.explanation: Leviticus 23:24 — "In the seventh month, in the first day of the month, shall ye have a sabbath, a memorial of blowing of trumpets, an holy convocation."

teens.question: What was commanded during the Day of Atonement on the 10th of the seventh month?
teens.option_a: A great feast and celebration
teens.option_b: Afflicting their souls, offering a fire offering, and doing no work
teens.option_c: Blowing trumpets all day long
teens.option_d: Marching around the tabernacle
teens.explanation: Leviticus 23:27 — "Also on the tenth day of this seventh month there shall be a day of atonement: it shall be an holy convocation unto you; and ye shall afflict your souls, and offer an offering made by fire unto the LORD."

adults.question: What theological concept is conveyed by the term "holy convocations" used for all the appointed feasts in Leviticus 23?
adults.option_a: Public holidays for rest only
adults.option_b: Sacred assemblies called by God — the calendar itself is holy, and God's people gather at His invitation to encounter Him
adults.option_c: Optional observances left to individual conscience
adults.option_d: Administrative meeting times for the priesthood
adults.explanation: Leviticus 23:2 — "The feasts of the LORD, which ye shall proclaim to be holy convocations, even these are my feasts." God owns the feasts — they are His appointments.

adults.question: How does the Feast of Firstfruits in Leviticus 23:10-11 typologically point to Christ's resurrection?
adults.option_a: It was only a harvest ceremony with no prophetic dimension
adults.option_b: The firstfruits sheaf waved on the day after the Sabbath prefigures Christ as the firstfruits of them that slept, raised on the first day of the week
adults.option_c: It predicted the day of Pentecost only
adults.option_d: Firstfruits only applies to the nation of Israel's future restoration
adults.explanation: Leviticus 23:11 is fulfilled in 1 Corinthians 15:20 — "But now is Christ risen from the dead, and become the firstfruits of them that slept."

adults.question: What is the theological connection between Pentecost (Feast of Weeks, Leviticus 23:16-17) and the events of Acts 2?
adults.option_a: It is coincidental — the feast had no prophetic significance
adults.option_b: The 50th day from Firstfruits when two wave loaves of leavened bread were offered typifies the outpouring of the Spirit on Jews and Gentiles at the birth of the Church
adults.option_c: Pentecost only commemorated the giving of the Law at Sinai
adults.option_d: The leavened loaves pointed to the corruption of the early Church
adults.explanation: Leviticus 23:17 — "two wave loaves...they shall be baken with leaven" — leaven can picture humanity; the Spirit came on the 50th day (Acts 2:1), the same feast day.

adults.question: What social justice provision was embedded within the harvest feast instructions of Leviticus 23:22?
adults.option_a: Tithes must be paid to the priests before any harvest
adults.option_b: Corners of the field and gleanings were to be left for the poor and the stranger
adults.option_c: Harvest celebrations required a community feast
adults.option_d: Farmers had to sell surplus grain at fixed prices
adults.explanation: Leviticus 23:22 — "And when ye reap the harvest of your land, thou shalt not make clean riddance of the corners of thy field...thou shalt leave them unto the poor, and to the stranger."

adults.question: What is the consequence prescribed in Leviticus 23:29-30 for not afflicting oneself on the Day of Atonement?
adults.option_a: A fine of five shekels of silver
adults.option_b: That soul shall be cut off from among the people and destroyed by God
adults.option_c: Seven days of exclusion from the congregation
adults.option_d: A doubled sin offering the following year
adults.explanation: Leviticus 23:29-30 — "For whatsoever soul it be that shall not be afflicted in that same day, he shall be cut off from among his people. And whatsoever soul it be that doeth any work in that same day, the same soul will I destroy."

adults.question: What does the Feast of Tabernacles command Israel to remember, and what is its eschatological dimension?
adults.option_a: Only the wilderness wandering, with no future application
adults.option_b: The wilderness sojourn and the frailty of human existence; eschatologically it points to the millennial kingdom rest when nations will celebrate (Zechariah 14:16)
adults.option_c: It was purely an agricultural harvest festival
adults.option_d: It foreshadowed only the return from Babylonian exile
adults.explanation: Leviticus 23:43 — "That your generations may know that I made the children of Israel to dwell in booths." Zechariah 14:16 shows nations keeping this feast in the messianic age.

adults.question: Why were two loaves of leavened bread (rather than unleavened) waved on Pentecost in Leviticus 23:17?
adults.option_a: Leaven was permitted in all wave offerings
adults.option_b: Scholars suggest leaven represents the sinful nature of humanity brought before God — the Church is not sinless but is accepted in Christ
adults.option_c: It was simply a practical change for easier baking
adults.option_d: Unleavened flour was unavailable during harvest season
adults.explanation: Leviticus 23:17 — "Ye shall bring out of your habitations two wave loaves...they shall be baken with leaven; they are the firstfruits unto the LORD." The leavened loaves distinguish Pentecost from Passover offerings.

adults.question: How does the structure of the seven feasts in Leviticus 23 form a prophetic timeline of redemptive history?
adults.option_a: They are only seasonal agricultural calendars with no redemptive meaning
adults.option_b: Passover, Unleavened Bread, and Firstfruits were fulfilled in Christ's death, burial, and resurrection; Pentecost in the Spirit's coming; Trumpets, Atonement, and Tabernacles await fulfillment at the Second Coming and kingdom
adults.option_c: Only Passover has prophetic significance
adults.option_d: The feasts were abolished at the cross with no future fulfillment
adults.explanation: Leviticus 23 — Paul affirms the typological fulfillment: "Christ our passover is sacrificed for us" (1 Cor 5:7), "Christ the firstfruits" (1 Cor 15:23), with the fall feasts awaiting consummation.

kids.question: What kind of oil was used to keep the lamp burning in the tabernacle?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 24:2 — "Command the children of Israel, that they bring unto thee pure oil olive beaten for the light, to cause the lamps to burn continually."

kids.question: How many loaves of bread were placed on the golden table before the LORD each week?
kids.explanation: Leviticus 24:5 — "And thou shalt take fine flour, and bake twelve cakes thereof."

kids.question: What happened to the man who cursed and blasphemed God's name?
kids.option_a: He was put out of the camp and stoned to death
kids.option_d: He was made to clean the tabernacle
kids.explanation: Leviticus 24:14 — "Bring forth him that hath cursed without the camp; and let all that heard him lay their hands upon his head, and let all the congregation stone him."

teens.question: How often was the bread of the presence to be set before the LORD, and who ate the old loaves?
teens.option_a: Every week on the Sabbath; Aaron and his sons ate the old bread in the holy place
teens.option_b: Every new moon; the Levites ate the old bread outside the camp
teens.option_c: Every day; only the high priest ate it
teens.option_d: Every month; it was burned as an offering
teens.explanation: Leviticus 24:8-9 — "Every sabbath he shall set it in order before the LORD continually...And it shall be Aaron's and his sons'; and they shall eat it in the holy place."

teens.question: What was unique about the mother of the blasphemer identified in Leviticus 24?
teens.option_a: She was a Canaanite woman
teens.option_b: She was an Israelite woman; his father was an Egyptian
teens.option_c: She was a Midianite who had converted
teens.option_d: She was from the tribe of Levi
teens.explanation: Leviticus 24:10-11 — "And the son of an Israelitish woman, whose father was an Egyptian, went out among the children of Israel."

teens.question: What law concerning personal injury does Leviticus 24:19-20 establish?
teens.option_a: The offender must pay double the damages
teens.option_b: As he hath caused a blemish in a man, so shall it be done to him — breach for breach, eye for eye, tooth for tooth
teens.option_c: The elders would determine compensation
teens.option_d: The offender was banished from Israel
teens.explanation: Leviticus 24:19-20 — "And if a man cause a blemish in his neighbour; as he hath done, so shall it be done to him; breach for breach, eye for eye, tooth for tooth."

teens.question: Did the law of the blasphemer apply only to Israelites or also to strangers?
teens.option_a: It applied equally to the stranger and the born Israelite alike
teens.option_b: Only to native-born Israelites
teens.option_c: Only to strangers since Israelites had special privilege
teens.option_d: It was left to priestly discretion
teens.explanation: Leviticus 24:16 — "And he that blasphemeth the name of the LORD, he shall surely be put to death, and all the congregation shall certainly stone him: as well the stranger, as he that is born in the land."

teens.question: What frankinscense was placed with the showbread, and what happened to it?
teens.option_a: Pure frankincense was laid on each row and burned as a memorial offering
teens.option_b: Frankincense was mixed into the dough before baking
teens.option_c: It was sprinkled on the bread and eaten with it
